texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Methods for calculating solvent enthalpy of vaporisation values by a molecular modelling technique - effect of charge and method
texts.text: Methods for calculating solvent enthalpy of vaporisation values by a molecular modelling technique - effect of charge and method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: B.J. Clifton, T. Cosgrove, M.R. Warne *
texts.text: B.J. Clifton, T. Cosgrove, M.R. Warne *

texts.label: text
texts.orig: School of Chemistry , Cantock ' s Close , Uni 6 ersity of Bristol , Bristol BS 8 1 TS , UK Received 30 November 1998; accepted 13 July 1999
texts.text: School of Chemistry , Cantock ' s Close , Uni 6 ersity of Bristol , Bristol BS 8 1 TS , UK Received 30 November 1998; accepted 13 July 1999

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The enthalpy of vaporisation ( D H vap ) of the solvents methane, ethane, butane, chloromethane, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, carbon disulphide, cyclohexane, and ethyl acetate have been calculated by a free space molecular modelling method. This technique is based on evaluating contact energies and co-ordination numbers using two commercial software packages (Polygraf™ and Cerius 2 ™). The total potential energy of the solvent is calculated by the use of the Dreiding II semi-empirical force field where non-bonded van der Waals interactions are modelled using a Lennard-Jone 12-6 potential. A bulk solvent (an isolated solvent shell with a co-ordination number of approximately 12) surrounding a central solvent molecule is created and the total energy is calculated. Re-calculating without the central molecule yields the solvent-solvent interaction energy and hence D H vap . The contact energy is pair-wise and additive and can be compared with literature values for D H vap . Three methods for generating solvent shells and a non-shell method are compared. For one method, the effect of charge generation is studied. The D H vap values calculated, show large discrepancies between the different shell methods used. However, values obtained by different charge generation methods are relatively similar. The coulombic term is therefore considered unimportant in the energy terms for the solvents studied here. Rather, the results show that the enthalpy of vaporisation is very sensitive to the co-ordination number, accompanying contact energy and spatial separation. The conclusion is, that because of the uncertainty in the co-ordination numbers, D H vap can only be estimated up to an accuracy of 20%. © 2000 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.
texts.text: The enthalpy of vaporisation ( D H vap ) of the solvents methane, ethane, butane, chloromethane, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, carbon disulphide, cyclohexane, and ethyl acetate have been calculated by a free space molecular modelling method. This technique is based on evaluating contact energies and co-ordination numbers using two commercial software packages (Polygraf™ and Cerius 2 ™). The total potential energy of the solvent is calculated by the use of the Dreiding II semi-empirical force field where non-bonded van der Waals interactions are modelled using a Lennard-Jone 12-6 potential. A bulk solvent (an isolated solvent shell with a co-ordination number of approximately 12) surrounding a central solvent molecule is created and the total energy is calculated. Re-calculating without the central molecule yields the solvent-solvent interaction energy and hence D H vap . The contact energy is pair-wise and additive and can be compared with literature values for D H vap . Three methods for generating solvent shells and a non-shell method are compared. For one method, the effect of charge generation is studied. The D H vap values calculated, show large discrepancies between the different shell methods used. However, values obtained by different charge generation methods are relatively similar. The coulombic term is therefore considered unimportant in the energy terms for the solvents studied here. Rather, the results show that the enthalpy of vaporisation is very sensitive to the co-ordination number, accompanying contact energy and spatial separation. The conclusion is, that because of the uncertainty in the co-ordination numbers, D H vap can only be estimated up to an accuracy of 20%. © 2000 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermodynamic properties of solvent based systems have been studied previously using lattice based models [1-3]. In contrast, in this study we examine if a more precise free space model can be used, and whether such a model provides a

texts.label: text
texts.orig: greater insight. The technique is evaluated by calculating the enthalpy of vaporisation for a group of solvents.
texts.text: greater insight. The technique is evaluated by calculating the enthalpy of vaporisation for a group of solvents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Individual solvent clusters : shells are studied by computer simulation using classical mechanical energy minimisation (MM). The total potential energy consisting of bonding, angle, angular torsion, inversion, van der Waals, hydrogen bonding and coulombic terms, is calculated by the use of a semi-empirical force field. The Dreiding II force field [4] is selected because of its usefulness in predicting structures for a wide range of molecules in biological, organic and main group inorganic systems. Non-bonded van der Waals interactions are modelled using the LennardJones 12-6 potential. Co-ordination numbers are obtained by use of the 'molecular silverware' algorithm of Blanco et al [5], and by generating clusters manually.
texts.text: Individual solvent clusters : shells are studied by computer simulation using classical mechanical energy minimisation (MM). The total potential energy consisting of bonding, angle, angular torsion, inversion, van der Waals, hydrogen bonding and coulombic terms, is calculated by the use of a semi-empirical force field. The Dreiding II force field [4] is selected because of its usefulness in predicting structures for a wide range of molecules in biological, organic and main group inorganic systems. Non-bonded van der Waals interactions are modelled using the LennardJones 12-6 potential. Co-ordination numbers are obtained by use of the 'molecular silverware' algorithm of Blanco et al [5], and by generating clusters manually.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Initial modelling experiments, conducted with and without partial charges, revealed that for some solvents, the magnitude of the charge as well as the method of charge generation, may be important. In this study, the effect of charge magnitude and method of generation is investigated by comparing the enthalpy of vaporisation using no charges, with that of three different charge generation techniques - Gasteiger [6], charge equilibration [7], and modified neglect of differential overlap [8].
texts.text: Initial modelling experiments, conducted with and without partial charges, revealed that for some solvents, the magnitude of the charge as well as the method of charge generation, may be important. In this study, the effect of charge magnitude and method of generation is investigated by comparing the enthalpy of vaporisation using no charges, with that of three different charge generation techniques - Gasteiger [6], charge equilibration [7], and modified neglect of differential overlap [8].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Simulation procedure
texts.text: 2. Simulation procedure

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Enthalpy of 6 aporisation
texts.text: 2.1. Enthalpy of 6 aporisation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here we consider D H vap to be the energy required to completely remove the central solvent molecule from it's surrounding shell into a vacuum. A cluster consisting of a solvated central molecule is generated either using the 'molecular silverware' algorithm or manually. The geometry is then optimised by minimisation using a conjugate gradient technique [9], until convergence is found (RMS gradient 0.4184 kJ mol 1 atom 1 ). Because we use molecular mechanics no temperature effects are taken into account. However, in this work this is unimportant because we are only interested in the intermolecular interactions. The energy of the cluster is determined followed by the energy of the shell once the central molecule is removed. Note that the shell is not minimised after the central molecule is deleted. D H vap is then obtained from:
texts.text: Here we consider D H vap to be the energy required to completely remove the central solvent molecule from it's surrounding shell into a vacuum. A cluster consisting of a solvated central molecule is generated either using the 'molecular silverware' algorithm or manually. The geometry is then optimised by minimisation using a conjugate gradient technique [9], until convergence is found (RMS gradient 0.4184 kJ mol 1 atom 1 ). Because we use molecular mechanics no temperature effects are taken into account. However, in this work this is unimportant because we are only interested in the intermolecular interactions. The energy of the cluster is determined followed by the energy of the shell once the central molecule is removed. Note that the shell is not minimised after the central molecule is deleted. D H vap is then obtained from:

texts.orig: D H vap 1 : 2 N A[(cluster energy) (shell energy)] (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For each pair of molecules, one contact is broken during the vaporisation process. Therefore for bulk solvent, a factor of two must be taken into account to avoid over counting the number of contacts. To the best of our knowledge, this approach has not been adopted before. However it is not clear how best to generate solvent clusters around a central molecule and hence four distinct methods are compared:
texts.text: For each pair of molecules, one contact is broken during the vaporisation process. Therefore for bulk solvent, a factor of two must be taken into account to avoid over counting the number of contacts. To the best of our knowledge, this approach has not been adopted before. However it is not clear how best to generate solvent clusters around a central molecule and hence four distinct methods are compared:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. The Blanco algorithms
texts.text: 2.2. The Blanco algorithms

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first three methods utilise the 'molecular silverware' algorithms of Blanco [5]. There are two types of algorithm; cluster and pair-wise. These are discussed below.
texts.text: The first three methods utilise the 'molecular silverware' algorithms of Blanco [5]. There are two types of algorithm; cluster and pair-wise. These are discussed below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once the initial structure of the central molecule has been constructed and optimised, the positions of nearest neighbours are determined by the 'cluster' algorithms of Blanco. The steps are as described below.
texts.text: Once the initial structure of the central molecule has been constructed and optimised, the positions of nearest neighbours are determined by the 'cluster' algorithms of Blanco. The steps are as described below.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The centres of mass of both molecules are positioned near the origin of a Cartesian coordinate frame (see Fig. 1a). The co-ordinates of molecule 1 (white) are unchanged throughout the calculation.
texts.text: The centres of mass of both molecules are positioned near the origin of a Cartesian coordinate frame (see Fig. 1a). The co-ordinates of molecule 1 (white) are unchanged throughout the calculation.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. A particular orientation of molecule 2 (dark grey) is determined by randomly choosing three Euler angles.
texts.text: A particular orientation of molecule 2 (dark grey) is determined by randomly choosing three Euler angles.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. A vector ( n ) that points from the origin to the surface of a unit sphere is randomly chosen.
texts.text: A vector ( n ) that points from the origin to the surface of a unit sphere is randomly chosen.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Molecule 2 is translated along the vector until the van der Waals surfaces of each molecule just touch each other.
texts.text: Molecule 2 is translated along the vector until the van der Waals surfaces of each molecule just touch each other.

Each nearest neighbour added must just touch the central molecule while avoiding overlapping the other nearest neighbours (see Figs. 1b-d). texts.orig: molecules will fit (Fig. 1e). This yields the co-ordination number Z ij . Z ij is defined as the number of molecules of type j that can be packed around a single molecule of type i. In this case the number of packing trials was set to its maximum of 1000 attempts. All orientations of the surrounding molecules with respect to the central molecule are determined randomly and no configurations were preferentially weighted in any way.
texts.text: molecules will fit (Fig. 1e). This yields the co-ordination number Z ij . Z ij is defined as the number of molecules of type j that can be packed around a single molecule of type i. In this case the number of packing trials was set to its maximum of 1000 attempts. All orientations of the surrounding molecules with respect to the central molecule are determined randomly and no configurations were preferentially weighted in any way.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the co-ordination number ( Z ), the Blanco algorithms can be used to generate molecular pairs. Steps 1-4 are followed as before, but instead of repeating the process to calculate Z ij , the energy of the interaction is determined and another pair generated. This is the 'pair-wise' form of the Blanco algorithm. By this method, the mean of 10 000 pairs gives the pair-wise interaction energy.
texts.text: In addition to the co-ordination number ( Z ), the Blanco algorithms can be used to generate molecular pairs. Steps 1-4 are followed as before, but instead of repeating the process to calculate Z ij , the energy of the interaction is determined and another pair generated. This is the 'pair-wise' form of the Blanco algorithm. By this method, the mean of 10 000 pairs gives the pair-wise interaction energy.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Energy methods
texts.text: 2.3. Energy methods

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.1. Pair -wise method
texts.text: 2.3.1. Pair -wise method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This method uses the Blanco pair-wise algorithm described above to determine the interaction energy of molecule pairs. The cluster algorithm is used for the corresponding co-ordination numbers. The enthalpy of vaporisation is obtained by multiplying the mean contact energy by the mean co-ordination number. This approach leaves holes in the solvation shell. As the form of the algorithm includes excluded volume ( V ex ) constraints, but does not take into account free volume ( V free ). It is therefore unclear what such holes represent. The energies calculated are that of a pair in contact, rather than in minimised contact.
texts.text: This method uses the Blanco pair-wise algorithm described above to determine the interaction energy of molecule pairs. The cluster algorithm is used for the corresponding co-ordination numbers. The enthalpy of vaporisation is obtained by multiplying the mean contact energy by the mean co-ordination number. This approach leaves holes in the solvation shell. As the form of the algorithm includes excluded volume ( V ex ) constraints, but does not take into account free volume ( V free ). It is therefore unclear what such holes represent. The energies calculated are that of a pair in contact, rather than in minimised contact.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.2. Cluster method
texts.text: 2.3.2. Cluster method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clusters are generated by the Blanco cluster algorithm and the average value of Z determined. The contact energy is calculated by measuring the total energy of the cluster and then recalculating without the central molecule. This difference is then D H vap . As with the pair-wise method, the energy calculated is that of a pair in contact, rather than minimised, and again holes are apparent in the clusters (see Fig. 2).
texts.text: Clusters are generated by the Blanco cluster algorithm and the average value of Z determined. The contact energy is calculated by measuring the total energy of the cluster and then recalculating without the central molecule. This difference is then D H vap . As with the pair-wise method, the energy calculated is that of a pair in contact, rather than minimised, and again holes are apparent in the clusters (see Fig. 2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.3. Minimised cluster method
texts.text: 2.3.3. Minimised cluster method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Clusters are generated by the Blanco cluster algorithm, and the configuration is then minimised. The contact energy is calculated as for the cluster method (cluster-shell), giving a minimised energy, but the original holes, although reduced slightly by the minimisation, still remain.
texts.text: Clusters are generated by the Blanco cluster algorithm, and the configuration is then minimised. The contact energy is calculated as for the cluster method (cluster-shell), giving a minimised energy, but the original holes, although reduced slightly by the minimisation, still remain.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.4. Manual method
texts.text: 2.3.4. Manual method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As for the other methods, the Blanco cluster algorithm is used to generate a cluster. In order to eliminate the holes formed, the initial cluster is geometry minimised, and then additional molecules are packed using the Blanco algorithm. In this process the existing packing molecules are defined as excluded molecules. The limiting value is therefore a uniform compacted shell of solvent molecules around the central solvent molecule, as determined by minimisation. The total co-ordination number was judged by eye, i.e. when no more holes are apparent. This is illustrated in Fig. 3. The contact energy, D H vap , is obtained from the difference cluster-shell. In this case, the energy evaluated is a minimised one, with no holes in the structure. However, such a re-packing method does not consider the effects of V free . This is discussed later.
texts.text: As for the other methods, the Blanco cluster algorithm is used to generate a cluster. In order to eliminate the holes formed, the initial cluster is geometry minimised, and then additional molecules are packed using the Blanco algorithm. In this process the existing packing molecules are defined as excluded molecules. The limiting value is therefore a uniform compacted shell of solvent molecules around the central solvent molecule, as determined by minimisation. The total co-ordination number was judged by eye, i.e. when no more holes are apparent. This is illustrated in Fig. 3. The contact energy, D H vap , is obtained from the difference cluster-shell. In this case, the energy evaluated is a minimised one, with no holes in the structure. However, such a re-packing method does not consider the effects of V free . This is discussed later.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Charge generation methods
texts.text: 2.4. Charge generation methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to comparing the different energy methods for calculating D H vap , the 'manual' method was also chosen to study the sensitivity of different charge generation methods on the total energy. Three methods, Gasteiger [6], charge equilibration [7], and MNDO [8], are described in detail in the literature. The MNDO method used was via MOPAC6 [10]. A comparison with energies where the electrostatic term is completely ignored, i.e. no charges, was also made. In this case, atom charges were initially set to zero and not altered during the minimisation process. All other parameters remained identical.
texts.text: In addition to comparing the different energy methods for calculating D H vap , the 'manual' method was also chosen to study the sensitivity of different charge generation methods on the total energy. Three methods, Gasteiger [6], charge equilibration [7], and MNDO [8], are described in detail in the literature. The MNDO method used was via MOPAC6 [10]. A comparison with energies where the electrostatic term is completely ignored, i.e. no charges, was also made. In this case, atom charges were initially set to zero and not altered during the minimisation process. All other parameters remained identical.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effect of charge generation
texts.text: 3.1. Effect of charge generation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most significant error in the 'manual' method, is considered to be the estimation of the co-ordination number. For example, the solvated shells all have co-ordination numbers of approximately 12. By having one more or one less molecule in the shell, an error of the order of 10% results. This is considered as the basic accuracy of the method. The difference in magnitude of point charges calculated for ethyl acetate by the different charge generation methods is illustrated in Table 1.
texts.text: The most significant error in the 'manual' method, is considered to be the estimation of the co-ordination number. For example, the solvated shells all have co-ordination numbers of approximately 12. By having one more or one less molecule in the shell, an error of the order of 10% results. This is considered as the basic accuracy of the method. The difference in magnitude of point charges calculated for ethyl acetate by the different charge generation methods is illustrated in Table 1.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: B . J . Clifton et al . : Colloids and Surfaces A : Physicochem . Eng . Aspects 164 (2000) 163-173
texts.text: B . J . Clifton et al . : Colloids and Surfaces A : Physicochem . Eng . Aspects 164 (2000) 163-173

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All the methods calculate a large negative partial charge on the oxygen atoms, as would be expected, with positive charges on each carbon atom. Only the Gasteiger method predicts any significant difference in charge between the two types of oxygen atoms. On the basis of simple electronegativity theory, one would expect the magnitude of charges on the carbon atoms to decrease as C 2 \ C3 \ C1. This is found to be the case for both Gasteiger and MNDO methods. However, QEq gives C2 \ C1 \ C3, without apparent explanation. The QEq method also gives appreciably higher charges on the oxygen atoms than either MNDO or Gasteiger. QEq charge values for C O and C O are 40 and 60% higher than MNDO, respectively, and 100 and 20% higher than Gasteiger.
texts.text: All the methods calculate a large negative partial charge on the oxygen atoms, as would be expected, with positive charges on each carbon atom. Only the Gasteiger method predicts any significant difference in charge between the two types of oxygen atoms. On the basis of simple electronegativity theory, one would expect the magnitude of charges on the carbon atoms to decrease as C 2 \ C3 \ C1. This is found to be the case for both Gasteiger and MNDO methods. However, QEq gives C2 \ C1 \ C3, without apparent explanation. The QEq method also gives appreciably higher charges on the oxygen atoms than either MNDO or Gasteiger. QEq charge values for C O and C O are 40 and 60% higher than MNDO, respectively, and 100 and 20% higher than Gasteiger.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For ethyl acetate, the MNDO method is considered the most accurate charge generation method due to its more fundamental approach: the wave function for each atom is calculated explicitly. Gasteiger provides an empirical approximation of partial charges, which is in good agreement with that of MNDO. The charge equilibration method however, would appear to be at odds with expectation, particularly with respect to the relatively small positive charge on C 3 .
texts.text: For ethyl acetate, the MNDO method is considered the most accurate charge generation method due to its more fundamental approach: the wave function for each atom is calculated explicitly. Gasteiger provides an empirical approximation of partial charges, which is in good agreement with that of MNDO. The charge equilibration method however, would appear to be at odds with expectation, particularly with respect to the relatively small positive charge on C 3 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The charge equilibrated clusters of Fig. 4 show some quite inconsistent results. The predicted values for D H vap are considerably different to that of the experimental results, with only an indication of a positive slope. It was also discovered that the net total charge of some clusters after minimisation was as large as 2.5 e , as opposed to being neutral. Clearly the charge equilibration method was not suitable for such a range of molecules.
texts.text: The charge equilibrated clusters of Fig. 4 show some quite inconsistent results. The predicted values for D H vap are considerably different to that of the experimental results, with only an indication of a positive slope. It was also discovered that the net total charge of some clusters after minimisation was as large as 2.5 e , as opposed to being neutral. Clearly the charge equilibration method was not suitable for such a range of molecules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When comparing the different charge generation methods it can be seen that there is good agreement between Gasteiger, MNDO and the uncharged data. The fact that the uncharged method shows good agreement with other methods indicates that the vaporisation of these molecules is dominated by van der Waals interactions, while electrostatics play a minor role. This might be expected because of the low conductivity of the molecules chosen.
texts.text: When comparing the different charge generation methods it can be seen that there is good agreement between Gasteiger, MNDO and the uncharged data. The fact that the uncharged method shows good agreement with other methods indicates that the vaporisation of these molecules is dominated by van der Waals interactions, while electrostatics play a minor role. This might be expected because of the low conductivity of the molecules chosen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the Gasteiger and MNDO methods (Figs. 5 and 6, respectively), there are three exceptions to the trend of good fit: CCl4, CHCl3 and CH3Cl. These three solvents are all polar in nature. CCl 4 is polarisable, while CHCl 3 and CH3Cl are considered polar solvents. It could therefore be suggested that coulombic terms play a more important part in the total energy for these particular molecules. However, the same discrepancies are apparent when no charges are considered, again suggesting the source of error is not the method of charge generation.
texts.text: For the Gasteiger and MNDO methods (Figs. 5 and 6, respectively), there are three exceptions to the trend of good fit: CCl4, CHCl3 and CH3Cl. These three solvents are all polar in nature. CCl 4 is polarisable, while CHCl 3 and CH3Cl are considered polar solvents. It could therefore be suggested that coulombic terms play a more important part in the total energy for these particular molecules. However, the same discrepancies are apparent when no charges are considered, again suggesting the source of error is not the method of charge generation.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: B . J . Clifton et al . : Colloids and Surfaces A : Physicochem . Eng . Aspects 164 (2000) 163-173
texts.text: B . J . Clifton et al . : Colloids and Surfaces A : Physicochem . Eng . Aspects 164 (2000) 163-173

texts.label: text
texts.orig: cluster, and hence a large degree of inaccuracy in its energy. This is apparent with no charges (Fig. 7) and Gasteiger values (Fig. 5). Such a discrepancy would infer that the cluster structure itself, i.e. the packing of the central molecule, is more important in determining the enthalpy of vaporisation than the coulombic energy term. It is not known why the similarly irregular structure of vinylacetate agrees well with its corresponding experimental value, though this could be fortuitous. The use of the Amber force field parameters instead of those of Dreiding for the non-bonding terms did not alter the energy values for CCl4, CH3Cl and CHCl3 significantly.

The use of the Amber force field parameters instead of those of Dreiding for the non-bonding terms did not alter the energy values for CCl4, CH3Cl and CHCl3 significantly.

texts.orig: The results of the various charge generation methods led to the Gasteiger model being adopted for all subsequent charge calculations. The MNDO method was unfortunately restricted by the number of atoms allowed in the program

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (maximum 200), while the QEq technique clearly was not suitable for the molecules considered. As coulombic interactions are several orders of magnitude greater in range than vdW, it did not seem reasonable to simply ignore the charges completely. However, whichever method of charge generation is chosen, its effect on the total interaction energy is small. This is indicated by the goodness of fit of the 'no charges' method in Fig. 8. Because of this, the effect of altering the cut-off distance for electrostatic interactions was not investigated. The cut-off for the potential was set at 7.0 A , which was considered reasonable throughout.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: sponding experimental values in Figs. 8-10 (for the manual method see Fig. 5). In all cases partial charges were generated by the Gasteiger method. As can be seen from the plots, none of the methods provide a fit as good as the one obtained for the hand made clusters of Fig. 5. The conclusion is that the holes which result from the Blanco algorithms, as illustrated in Fig. 2, are incorrect (Figs. 9 and 10), and can not be representative of a solvent cluster. As for the pair-wise method of Fig. 8, it would seem appropriate to minimise each pair for a small number of steps before the next pair is generated, to represent equilibration. If this process could be automated, then it would not increase the calculation time significantly. Each cluster could still be calculated in a matter of minutes. Unfortunately, this method is not available within the program. Minimisation of each pair manually for 10 000 pairs was not considered viable. It could therefore be concluded that the manual method best represents a solvent shell from which the enthalpy of vaporisation can be calculated. The fact that none of the methods chosen to study solvent-solvent interactions could account for free volume, shows the limita-

texts.orig: tions of the technique, assuming errors in the force field are relatively small. It is thought this inability precludes the precise calculation of D H vap , rather than the methods themselves. The pair-wise, cluster, and minimised cluster methods can therefore be considered as underestimating D H vap , due to the appearance of holes (even though free volume effects are not included in the methods). The manual method may overestimate D H vap , as free volume is minimised by re-packing.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. General obser 6 ations
texts.text: 4.1. General obser 6 ations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The largest error expected for the molecular modelling technique is that due to the packing of solvent molecules around a central molecule. This is estimated as 10% for the different charge generation methods and 20-45% for the different energy methods, concluded from the best line of fit obtained for the enthalpy of vaporisation calculations. The results of the four energy methods to investigate solvent-solvent interactions gave a wide range of values, with the best fit estimated to be by the manual technique. However, the unexpected results given by the 'molecular silverware' algorithms were a significant problem. The pairWise technique of Blanco would seem to have the greatest potential. Generating thousands of molecular pairs (maximum 10 000) allows an average interaction energy to be obtained within minutes. However, to represent an equilibrium energy, each pair must be minimised. All the methods relied upon an accurate co-ordination

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Charge methods
texts.text: 4.2. Charge methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Enthalpy of vaporisation calculations provided a useful way of comparing and testing the different approaches used to evaluate the partial charges of a system. The results show that the most likely method for charge generation to be chosen (QEq), was in fact a poor choice. However it would seem, at least for the solvents studied, that the inclusion of partial charges for the enthalpy of vaporisation, was not a major factor.
texts.text: Enthalpy of vaporisation calculations provided a useful way of comparing and testing the different approaches used to evaluate the partial charges of a system. The results show that the most likely method for charge generation to be chosen (QEq), was in fact a poor choice. However it would seem, at least for the solvents studied, that the inclusion of partial charges for the enthalpy of vaporisation, was not a major factor.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Energy methods
texts.text: 4.3. Energy methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Enthalpy of vaporisation calculations were further employed to compare the different methods for determining polymer-solvent interactions. None of the methods studied would appear to give a definitive answer as to the best approach. However, minimisation would seem to be appropriate, as those methods (minimised cluster, manual) show better fits compared with experimental values. The conclusion is that the manual method overestimates the solvent-solvent energy, while the other methods underestimate its value.
texts.text: Enthalpy of vaporisation calculations were further employed to compare the different methods for determining polymer-solvent interactions. None of the methods studied would appear to give a definitive answer as to the best approach. However, minimisation would seem to be appropriate, as those methods (minimised cluster, manual) show better fits compared with experimental values. The conclusion is that the manual method overestimates the solvent-solvent energy, while the other methods underestimate its value.
